name: Lono
description: Lono is a prominent deity in Hawaiian mythology, associated with fertility, agriculture, peace, and music. He is one of the four great gods (Kū, Kāne, Kanaloa, and Lono) who existed before the creation of the world. Lono's origins are often shrouded in mystery, with some accounts claiming he descended from the heavens or emerged from the depths of the ocean. He is revered as a bringer of life and prosperity, ensuring the abundance of crops and the well-being of the land. His powers encompass the ability to control weather patterns, particularly rainfall, essential for successful agriculture. Lono's role in Hawaiian society was crucial, as his blessings were sought for bountiful harvests and peaceful times. The Makahiki festival, a four-month-long celebration, was dedicated to him, during which warfare and heavy labor were forbidden, and games, feasting, and worship honored his name. Lono is also associated with healing and reconciliation, embodying the restorative forces of nature. He is sometimes depicted as a traveler who sailed the seas, bringing knowledge and gifts to different islands. The story of his tragic relationship with his wife, Kaikilani, leading to his departure from Hawai'i is a notable aspect of his mythology. His return was prophesied, often associated with the arrival of Captain Cook, leading to complex interpretations of his role in Hawaiian history. Lono's influence extends beyond agriculture to encompass the arts, particularly music and dance, making him a multifaceted and deeply revered figure in Hawaiian culture. He is also associated with the stars and the cycles of the seasons, reinforcing his connection to the natural world and its rhythms.
subtitle: God of fertility, peace, and agriculture

keyMyths.title: Lono and Kaikilani
keyMyths.description: Lono, overcome with grief after suspecting his wife Kaikilani of infidelity, traveled the islands and initiated games and festivals to honor her. This eventually led to the Makahiki season.

keyMyths.title: The Makahiki Festival
keyMyths.description: The Makahiki festival was a four-month-long celebration dedicated to Lono, during which warfare was forbidden and people engaged in sports, games, and feasting. It celebrated the harvest and the coming of peace.
keyMyths.source: Kamakau, Samuel Manaiakalani. Ruling Chiefs of Hawaii.

keyMyths.title: Lono's Prophecy
keyMyths.description: A prophecy foretold Lono's return, often associated with a ship carrying a white sail. When Captain Cook arrived, some Hawaiians initially believed he was Lono, leading to complex interactions.
keyMyths.source: Obookiah, Henry. Memoirs of Henry Obookiah.

sources.author: Martha Warren Beckwith
sources.description: A comprehensive collection of Hawaiian myths and legends.
sources.text: A comprehensive collection of Hawaiian myths and legends.
